name: Sierra del Rincón Biosphere Reserve Loop
name_original: Puerto de La Hiruela y Embalse de Puentes Viejas — circular por el Somosierra
description: This difficult 66.9-mile road cycling route offers challenging climbs, stunning mountain scenery, and picturesque villages in the Sierra del
region_intro: Road cycling around Campillo De Ranas features mountainous terrain within the Sierra Norte de Guadalajara Natural Park, characterized by significant elevation changes and sustained climbs. The region offers diverse landscapes, including dense pine and oak forests, expansive pastures, and mountain ranges like the Sierra de Puebla and Sierra del Rincón. Road cyclists can expect challenging routes with low vehicle traffic, providing a tranquil experience through scenic mountain roads and valleys. Best road cycling routes around Campillo De Ranas The most popular road cycling route is Sierra del Rincón Biosphere Reserve Loop, a 66.9 miles (107.7 km) trail that takes 6 hours 10 minutes to complete. This difficult route traverses the protected Biosphere Reserve, offering extensive mountain views. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Port of La Puebla and Atazar Reservoir — circular via Somosierra, a difficult 94.1 miles (151.4 km) path. This route includes major climbs and scenic sections around the Atazar Reservoir. Local road cyclists also love the Black Architecture Villages Loop, a 27.1 miles (43.6 km) trail leading through traditional "black architecture" villages, often completed in about 2 hours 17 minutes. Road cycling around Campillo De Ranas is defined by mountainous passes, dense forests, and unique "black architecture" villages. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate routes to challenging climbs. The routes in Campillo De Ranas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 270 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Campillo De Ranas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Campillo de Ranas?
A: There are over 10 dedicated road cycling routes around Campillo de Ranas, offering diverse experiences within the Sierra Norte de Guadalajara Natural Park. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars.

Q: What is the typical terrain like for road cycling in Campillo de Ranas?
A: Road cycling in Campillo de Ranas is characterized by mountainous terrain with significant elevation changes and sustained climbs. You'll encounter diverse landscapes, including dense pine and oak forests, expansive pastures, and scenic mountain ranges like the Sierra de Puebla and Sierra del Rincón. Many routes feature well-paved surfaces, making them suitable for road bikes.

Q: How challenging are the road cycling routes in Campillo de Ranas?
A: The routes in Campillo de Ranas offer a range of challenges. While there are some moderate options, the majority are considered difficult, featuring substantial elevation gains. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017110366/port-of-la-puebla-and-atazar-reservoir-circular-via-somosierra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of La Puebla and Atazar Reservoir — circular via Somosierra&lt;/a&gt; route includes major climbs with over 2,800 meters of ascent. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017126859/climb-to-puerto-de-la-quesera-round-trip-from-campillo-de-ranas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Puerto de la Quesera&lt;/a&gt; are particularly demanding, known for their inclusion in major cycling events.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Campillo de Ranas?
A: While many routes in Campillo de Ranas are challenging, there are some moderate options available. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1375081285/black-architecture-villages-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Black Architecture Villages Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, is a moderate 43.6 km route that allows for a more relaxed pace while still enjoying the region's unique cultural sights. It's always recommended to check the route's difficulty and elevation profile before setting out.

Q: What natural features can I expect to see while road cycling in Campillo de Ranas?
A: You'll cycle through the stunning Sierra Norte de Guadalajara Natural Park, offering views of prominent peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5431320" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Ocejón&lt;/a&gt;. The region is rich with dense pine and oak forests, expansive pastures, and picturesque mountain ranges. You might also encounter the Jarama River and its historic Trillos Bridge, or pass by reservoirs such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6619443" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vado Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical landmarks along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, Campillo de Ranas is famous for its 'black architecture' villages, built primarily from dark slate. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1375081285/black-architecture-villages-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Black Architecture Villages Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically takes you through these traditional settlements. You can also find historical sites like an ancient Roman bridge, which is a popular spot for photography, and the Church of Nuestra Señora del Rosario in Campillo de Ranas itself.

Q: Where can I find the best viewpoints on a road cycling route?
A: Many routes offer spectacular panoramic vistas of the Sierra Norte. The viewpoint of Fuente de las Ranas provides breathtaking views of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5431320" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Ocejón&lt;/a&gt;. Routes that traverse mountain passes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3080243" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de la Puebla&lt;/a&gt;, also offer extensive mountain views and scenic spots, particularly through dense oak groves.

Q: Are the road cycling routes in Campillo de Ranas generally low-traffic?
A: Yes, one of the significant appeals of road cycling in Campillo de Ranas is the low vehicle traffic on many roads. Routes like the GU-186 towards Campillo de Ranas and Majaelrayo, and the GU-211 to Valverde de los Arroyos, are known for their tranquility, allowing cyclists to enjoy the picturesque mountain roads with minimal disturbance.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Campillo de Ranas?
A: The best time for road cycling in Campillo de Ranas is typically during spring and autumn when the weather is milder and the landscapes are vibrant. Summer can be quite warm, especially at lower elevations, while winter may bring colder temperatures and potential snow in the higher mountain passes, making some routes less accessible.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling options in the area?
A: Yes, Campillo de Ranas offers several long-distance road cycling routes for those seeking extended challenges. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017110366/port-of-la-puebla-and-atazar-reservoir-circular-via-somosierra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of La Puebla and Atazar Reservoir — circular via Somosierra&lt;/a&gt; is a significant 151.4 km path, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017119476/sierra-del-rincon-biosphere-reserve-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra del Rincón Biosphere Reserve Loop&lt;/a&gt; covers 107.7 km, both offering extensive mountain views and demanding climbs.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay near the cycling routes in Campillo de Ranas?
A: Campillo de Ranas and the surrounding 'black architecture' villages offer various options for accommodation, including rural houses and small hotels. You'll also find local restaurants and cafes where you can refuel with traditional Spanish cuisine after your ride. It's advisable to check availability, especially during peak seasons.
tips: Nice descent that goes from right to left and where from the other side of the Jaramilla River you can see this postcard image, of a movie road!
The surroundings of this Madrid town are an extraordinary place to go on a bike route, especially during autumn, due to the abundance of deciduous trees.
Its name means Land of Dominion. Traditional houses, with corral and hayloft, alternate in the center and the periphery of the town with more modern houses.

The Castle and Walls of Buitrago del Lozoya, one of the most important medieval sites in the Community of Madrid:
The town has pre-Roman roots and was mentioned by Livy as a Celtiberian fortress (Litabrum).
Muslim Fortification: Between the 8th and 11th centuries, the Muslims built the wall to protect the territory.
Reconquest: Alfonso VI conquered it in 1085, and it passed into Christian hands.
Current Castle: Built in the 15th century by the Mendoza family, during the reign of John II of Castile.
Historical Uses: Noble residence, defensive stronghold, and setting for events such as the imprisonment of Juana la Beltraneja.

The Puentes Viejas reservoir was built on the Lozoya River and is part of the water supply network known as Canal de Isabel II, which is still in operation after several centuries in use. It is also a panoramic site with very nice views.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is considered difficult, designed for experienced road cyclists with good to very good fitness. It features significant elevation changes, including challenging mountain passes like the Puerto de La Hiruela, which has a demanding climb of 6.5 km with an average gradient of 5.4%.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: You will encounter high mountain landscapes, deep valleys, and forested areas. While many roads, especially on mountain passes, have good asphalt, be aware that some segments, even on routes categorized for road biking, may be unpaved and difficult to ride. Expect a constant mix of ascents and descents throughout the loop.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a variety of scenic and historical highlights. You'll pass through picturesque settlements like Montejo de la Sierra and La Serna del Monte. Other notable spots include the Buitrago del Lozoya Castle and Walls, the Puentes Viejas Dam, and a scenic Descent to the Río Jaramilla.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de Guadarrama region is best enjoyed during the spring and autumn months. These seasons offer more moderate temperatures and vibrant natural scenery. Summer can be very hot, especially at lower elevations, while winter may bring snow and ice to the higher passes, making cycling challenging or impossible.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or permits needed for cycling in the Sierra del Rincón Biosphere Reserve?
faqs.answer: The route passes through the Sierra del Rincón Biosphere Reserve. Generally, no specific permits are required for cycling on established roads. However, it's crucial to respect local regulations, stay on marked paths, and practice Leave No Trace principles to protect the natural environment. There are no entrance fees for the reserve itself.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for dogs?
faqs.answer: While the route is primarily on roads, which are generally accessible, it is a challenging road cycling tour. Bringing dogs along on a bicycle, especially for such a long and difficult route, is not typically recommended. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured and that you comply with any local leash laws or regulations in villages and protected areas.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route, you can typically start and park in one of the villages along the way, such as La Hiruela or Somosierra. Parking availability may vary, especially on weekends or holidays, so arriving early is advisable. Look for designated parking areas within the villages.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known trails or cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly overlaps with sections of several other paths. You'll find yourself on parts of the [CIMA MA10] La Puebla * La Hiruela, [CIMA GU04] Peñalba * La Hiruela, and the [CIMA MA10] La Puebla * Prádena del Rincón. It also touches segments of the GR-88 and El Barranco de El Jarama.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or get refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, you'll pass through several villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, or small shops. La Hiruela itself is known for its charming atmosphere and traditional architecture, offering options for a break. Montejo de la Sierra and Buitrago del Lozoya are also good spots to refuel.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, bring plenty of water and snacks, as well as appropriate cycling gear for varying weather conditions. A repair kit, spare tubes, and a pump are essential. A GPS device with the route loaded is highly recommended, as is a fully charged phone. Don't forget sun protection and layers of clothing.

faqs.question: Is public transport an option to reach the starting point of the route?
faqs.answer: Public transport options to the villages in the Sierra Norte de Madrid, such as La Hiruela or Somosierra, are limited, especially for cyclists with bikes. While some bus lines serve these areas, schedules can be infrequent. It's generally more convenient to travel by car to the starting point.

waypoints.name: Buitrago del Lozoya Castle and Walls
